content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>

<html:p>Which loop style is "best"? This very question led to the creation of Compiler Explorer! In 2011 I was arguing with my team about whether we could switch all our loops from ordinal or iterator-style to the "new" range-for<html:sup id="fnref:java"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:java">1</html:a></html:sup>. I wrote a small <html:a href="https://mattgodbolt.github.io/ce-behind-the-scenes/#/1/3">shell script</html:a> to iteratively show the compiler output as I edited code in <html:code>vim</html:code>, and the seed of <html:a href="https://godbolt.org">Compiler Explorer</html:a> was born.</html:p>

<html:p>C and C++ have many ways to phrase loops: <html:code>for()</html:code>, <html:code>while()</html:code>, <html:code>do..while()</html:code>, range-for (<html:code>for (x : container)</html:code>), STL algorithms like <html:code>std::for_each</html:code>, and now range transformations! Let's see if loop style actually matters for performance.</html:p>

<html:p>Let's look at similar functions that calculate the sum of a <html:code>std::vector<int></html:code>, using different looping strategies. First, let's use the ordinal-based approach:</html:p>

<html:p>To explain the generated assembly code, we need to take a look at the innards of a <html:code>std::vector</html:code><html:sup id="fnref:atleast"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:atleast">2</html:a></html:sup>. Internally, the vector holds three pointers: one to the beginning of the allocated data ("start"), one to the just past the end of the used data ("finish"), and a last one to the end of the storage (the space which we can grow into without reallocating). It does not explicitly store the size. Let's look at the first few instructions:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">QWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">      </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rsi = vec->start</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rcx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">QWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="mi">8</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rcx = vec->finish</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">sub</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rcx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rcx = finish - start</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">je</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L4</html:span><html:span class="w">                        </html:span><html:span class="c1">; if zero; early return</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">sar</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rcx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">2</html:span><html:span class="w">                    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rcx >>= 2 (divide by sizeof(int))</html:span>
<html:span class="w">                                </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rcx = vec.size()</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">xor</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; eax = 0 (this will be "index")</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">xor</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; edx = 0 (this will be "sum")</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>The first thing we do is get the start and finish pointers of the vector, and then subtract them to work out the size (returning early if it's zero). So far so good! (The .L4 label just returns 0, so I'll ignore it in this description).</html:p>

<html:p>Next we see the loop itself:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nl">.L3:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">*</html:span><html:span class="mi">4</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="c1">; edx += *(int*)(start + index*4)</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">1</html:span><html:span class="w">                    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ++index</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rcx</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; if index == size?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jb</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L3</html:span><html:span class="w">                        </html:span><html:span class="c1">; if not, loop</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; return "sum"</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ret</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>Here we see the slightly bonkers x86 addressing mode we touched on in the <html:a href="/202512/02-adding-integers">earlier lea post</html:a> which lets us read a value from memory calculated from the start plus the index (times four, as each <html:code>int</html:code> is four bytes), <html:em>and</html:em> add it in a single instruction. CISC, am I right?</html:p>

<html:p>Anyway, everything seems ok in this loop, right? Except, it bothers me that we calculate the size at all. I mean, we don't <html:em>really</html:em> care what size it is, we just want to iterate over every element, right?<html:sup id="fnref:raxret"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:raxret">3</html:a></html:sup></html:p>

<html:p>How about we rephrase this to use the pointers directly? We can fish out the start pointer with <html:code>.data()</html:code>, and calculate the end pointer by adding the size (and hope the optimiser spots it doesn't need to <html:em>actually</html:em> use the size, it can just use the <html:code>end</html:code> directly). What does that look like?</html:p>

<html:p>That's <html:em>much</html:em> better! The optimiser <html:em>has</html:em> avoided all the shifts and size calculation in the setup code, and the inner loop is simply reading and walking a pointer forward.</html:p>

<html:p>Any time you see C++ code with naked pointers in it, you should ask yourself if there's a better way, though. So, let's see what would happen if we used the fancy range-for:</html:p>

<html:p><html:strong>Exactly</html:strong> the same code as our <html:code>while</html:code>-based, pointer approach! The compiler canonicalised the range-for into identical assembly. Less error-prone pointer manipulation, more intention-revealing C++ code, <html:em>and</html:em> the best code generation!</html:p>

<html:p>Even with a standard algorithm, the pattern continues:</html:p>

<html:p>Identical again! Canonicalisation rewrites all these different loop forms into the same basic setup, generating optimal code every time<html:sup id="fnref:except"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:except">4</html:a></html:sup>. Whether you write explicit loops or use standard algorithms, the compiler sees through to the underlying iteration pattern. Write clear, intention-revealing code - the optimiser has your back.</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>See <html:a href="https://youtu.be/FB8Hgj3TpJM">the video</html:a> that accompanies this post.</html:em></html:p>

<html:hr />

<html:p><html:em>This post is day 8 of <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025</html:a>,
a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>← <html:a href="/202512/07-division-again">Multiplying our way out of division</html:a> | <html:a href="/202512/09-induction-variables">Induction variables and loops</html:a> →</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>Support Compiler Explorer on <html:a href="https://patreon.com/c/mattgodbolt">Patreon</html:a>
or <html:a href="https://github.com/sponsors/compiler-explorer">GitHub</html:a>,
or by buying CE products in the <html:a href="https://shop.compiler-explorer.com">Compiler Explorer Shop</html:a></html:em>.</html:p>

<html:div class="footnote">
<html:hr />
<html:ol>
<html:li id="fn:java">
<html:p>That might seem odd - why would we expect it to be slower? In this instance we had just been bitten by a similar issue in Java - a range-for equivalent in Java creates a temporary iteration object each time around, and we were trying to write "garbage free" java. This caught us out, so the team was a bit sensitive to this kind of issue. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:java" title="Jump back to footnote 1 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:atleast">
<html:p>At least, the GNU implementation. As best I know all standard library implementations follow this pattern, but I don't think the C++ standard requires them to work exactly this way. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:atleast" title="Jump back to footnote 2 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:raxret">
<html:p>Honestly it also annoys me the register allocator didn't put <html:code>sum</html:code> into <html:code>rax</html:code> from the start to save on the <html:code>mov eax, edx</html:code> at the end, but that's minor. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:raxret" title="Jump back to footnote 3 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:except">
<html:p>With the exception of the ordinal-based one where the compiler doesn't work out it can drop the loop index. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:except" title="Jump back to footnote 4 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
</html:ol>
</html:div>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. Which loop style is "best"? This very question led to the creation of Compiler Explorer! In 2011 I was arguing with my team about whether we could switch all our loops from ordinal or iterator-style to the "new" range-for 1 . I wrote a small shell script to iteratively show the compiler output as I edited code in vim , and the seed of Compiler Explorer was born. C and C++ have many ways to phrase loops: for() , while() , do..while() , range-for ( for (x : container) ), STL algorithms like std::for_each , and now range transformations! Let's see if loop style actually matters for performance. Let's look at similar functions that calculate the sum of a std::vector<int> , using different looping strategies. First, let's use the ordinal-based approach: To explain the generated assembly code, we need to take a look at the innards of a std::vector 2 . Internally, the vector holds three pointers: one to the beginning of the allocated data ("start"), one to the just past the end of the used data ("finish"), and a last one to the end of the storage (the space which we can grow into without reallocating). It does not explicitly store the size. Let's look at the first few instructions: mov rsi , QWORD PTR [ rdi ] ; rsi = vec->start mov rcx , QWORD PTR [ rdi + 8 ] ; rcx = vec->finish sub rcx , rsi ; rcx = finish - start je .L4 ; if zero; early return sar rcx , 2 ; rcx >>= 2 (divide by sizeof(int)) ; rcx = vec.size() xor eax , eax ; eax = 0 (this will be "index") xor edx , edx ; edx = 0 (this will be "sum") The first thing we do is get the start and finish pointers of the vector, and then subtract them to work out the size (returning early if it's zero). So far so good! (The .L4 label just returns 0, so I'll ignore it in this description). Next we see the loop itself: .L3: add edx , DWORD PTR [ rsi + rax * 4 ] ; edx += *(int*)(start + index*4) add rax , 1 ; ++index cmp rax , rcx ; if index == size? jb .L3 ; if not, loop mov eax , edx ; return "sum" ret Here we see the slightly bonkers x86 addressing mode we touched on in the earlier lea post which lets us read a value from memory calculated from the start plus the index (times four, as each int is four bytes), and add it in a single instruction. CISC, am I right? Anyway, everything seems ok in this loop, right? Except, it bothers me that we calculate the size at all. I mean, we don't really care what size it is, we just want to iterate over every element, right? 3 How about we rephrase this to use the pointers directly? We can fish out the start pointer with .data() , and calculate the end pointer by adding the size (and hope the optimiser spots it doesn't need to actually use the size, it can just use the end directly). What does that look like? That's much better! The optimiser has avoided all the shifts and size calculation in the setup code, and the inner loop is simply reading and walking a pointer forward. Any time you see C++ code with naked pointers in it, you should ask yourself if there's a better way, though. So, let's see what would happen if we used the fancy range-for: Exactly the same code as our while -based, pointer approach! The compiler canonicalised the range-for into identical assembly. Less error-prone pointer manipulation, more intention-revealing C++ code, and the best code generation! Even with a standard algorithm, the pattern continues: Identical again! Canonicalisation rewrites all these different loop forms into the same basic setup, generating optimal code every time 4 . Whether you write explicit loops or use standard algorithms, the compiler sees through to the underlying iteration pattern. Write clear, intention-revealing code - the optimiser has your back. See the video that accompanies this post. This post is day 8 of Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025 , a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code. ← Multiplying our way out of division | Induction variables and loops → This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans. Support Compiler Explorer on Patreon or GitHub , or by buying CE products in the Compiler Explorer Shop . That might seem odd - why would we expect it to be slower? In this instance we had just been bitten by a similar issue in Java - a range-for equivalent in Java creates a temporary iteration object each time around, and we were trying to write "garbage free" java. This caught us out, so the team was a bit sensitive to this kind of issue. ↩ At least, the GNU implementation. As best I know all standard library implementations follow this pattern, but I don't think the C++ standard requires them to work exactly this way. ↩ Honestly it also annoys me the register allocator didn't put sum into rax from the start to save on the mov eax, edx at the end, but that's minor. ↩ With the exception of the ordinal-based one where the compiler doesn't work out it can drop the loop index. ↩
summary: Exploring the ways optimisers deal with loop constructs
title: Going loopy
